food_name: Daging Hitam (Beef in Dark Soy Gravy)

ontology.foodex2_description: Beef, cooked — used as the closest FoodEx2 anchor for a slow-cooked beef dish in dark soy gravy; no specific Daging Hitam code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a specific entry for Daging Hitam or Malay dark-soy beef preparations; A0CKP (cooked beef) used as the nearest defensible anchor.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Daging Hitam is approximately 200 g, representing a generous portion of beef pieces coated in thick dark soy gravy as served alongside Nasi Kandar, Nasi Briyani, or plain white rice.

nutrient_source.title: Malaysian Food Composition Database (MyFCD) — Beef dishes, cooked; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy beef braised entries for fatty acid and micronutrient detail
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research (IMR) Malaysia / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact Daging Hitam entry exists in MyFCD. Values are adapted from MyFCD cooked beef dish entries and USDA SR Legacy braised beef data, adjusted for the characteristic high-soy-sauce and sugar content of Daging Hitam. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy beef chuck braised (USDA FDC ID 174032). Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat set to -1 as detailed fatty acid breakdown for this specific preparation is not available in either MyFCD or USDA FoodData Central for this dish. Cholesterol estimated from USDA SR Legacy braised beef. Input record values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.9) were cross-checked against MyFCD and USDA ranges and retained where consistent.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — analogous estimate based on beef and soy sauce mixed dish; GI of mixed beef-soy dish estimated at 45 (low) based on high protein and fat content moderating glucose absorption
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Daging Hitam. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on: (1) the University of Sydney GI Database entries for mixed beef dishes and soy-sauce-based preparations, which consistently fall in the low-to-medium range (GI 40–55); (2) the moderating effect of high protein (14.5 g/100g) and fat (15.2 g/100g) on glucose absorption; (3) the sugar content (8.5 g/100g) from kicap manis which contributes some glycemic load but is offset by the protein-fat matrix. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 25.0) / 100 = 11.25, rounded to 11.3.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (450 mg/100g; 900 mg per 200g serving) from sweet soy sauce (kicap manis) and dark soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Daging Hitam to small portions (≤100 g) and request reduced soy sauce preparation, as a standard 200 g serving provides approximately 900 mg sodium — nearly 40% of the 2300 mg daily limit recommended by the American Heart Association.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate sugar content (8.5 g/100g) from kicap manis and dark soy sauce, partially offset by high protein and fat slowing glucose absorption
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes may consume Daging Hitam in moderate portions (100–150 g) as the high protein and fat content attenuates postprandial glucose rise; however, total carbohydrate intake from accompanying rice must be carefully controlled to keep the overall meal glycemic load within target.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat (~5.8 g/100g) and high sodium (450 mg/100g) from beef and soy sauce components
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit Daging Hitam to occasional consumption, choose lean beef cuts, and request reduced soy sauce to lower both saturated fat and sodium intake, as a 200 g serving contributes approximately 11.6 g saturated fat — over half the 20 g daily limit for a 2000 kcal diet.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Daging Hitam, literally 'black meat' in Malay, is a classic Northern Malaysian beef dish most closely associated with Penang's Nasi Kandar culture and traditional Malay wedding feasts. The dish is defined by its deep, almost black, sticky caramelized gravy achieved through the generous use of sweet soy sauce (kicap manis) and dark soy sauce, which are slowly reduced with the beef until they form a thick, glossy glaze. The beef is slow-cooked with whole spices — star anise, cinnamon sticks, and cardamom pods — along with a pounded rempah paste of ginger, garlic, and shallots, producing a complex, layered flavor profile that is simultaneously savory, sweet, and warmly spiced. The long braising process renders the beef tender and deeply infused with the spice-soy matrix. Per 100 g, Daging Hitam provides approximately 245 kcal, 14.5 g protein, 15.2 g fat (including ~5.8 g saturated fat), and 12.5 g carbohydrate with 8.5 g sugar. Sodium is notably high at 450 mg/100g due to the dual soy sauce base. It is a rich source of vitamin B12 (1.2 mcg/100g), zinc (2.8 mg/100g), iron (2.2 mg/100g), and niacin (4.5 mg/100g) from the beef component. Clinically, the dish is high concern for hypertension due to sodium load, moderate concern for cardiovascular disease due to saturated fat, and moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to sugar from kicap manis — though the protein-fat matrix moderates postprandial glucose response. It is best served as a main dish alongside Nasi Briyani, Nasi Minyak, or plain white rice.

confidence_reason: No exact Daging Hitam entry exists in MyFCD or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from MyFCD cooked beef dish entries and USDA SR Legacy braised beef data, cross-checked against the input record (ai_generated, confidence 0.9). Core macros are consistent with expected ranges for a slow-cooked beef dish with soy sauce. GI is an analogous estimate. Saturated fat and cholesterol are estimated from USDA SR Legacy braised beef. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and water content are set to -1 due to absence of detailed fatty acid and moisture data for this specific preparation in approved sources.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown and moisture data for Daging Hitam (slow-cooked beef in dark soy gravy) are not available in MyFCD (IMR Malaysia) or USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026. Saturated fat (5.8 g/100g) and cholesterol (55 mg/100g) are estimated from USDA SR Legacy beef chuck braised (FDC ID 174032) and adjusted for the soy sauce and oil contribution. All core macros (protein_g 14.5, fat_g 15.2, carbohydrate_g 12.5) are non-zero and consistent with a slow-cooked beef dish with soy sauce and sugar; no zero-macro justification required. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database mixed beef-soy dish entries; no direct GI measurement for Daging Hitam exists in the published literature. Glycemic load per serving = (45 × 25.0 g carbohydrate per 200g serving) / 100 = 11.3. The input source citation ('Nutrient Composition of Northern Malaysian Beef Dishes', HPB Singapore) was not verifiable as a specific HPB publication; MyFCD and USDA SR Legacy were used as the primary approved sources instead. Tamil and Hindi local names are not applicable as this is a Malaysian Malay dish, not a Singaporean dish; the G6 multilingual rule for Singaporean dishes does not apply.
